summary: Nadia explores the tension between sharing interesting discoveries and protecting fragile communities from unwanted attention. She draws parallels between NIMBYism in physical cities and the 'GET OFF MY BOARD' mentality of online subcultures, arguing that both stem from a legitimate desire to preserve community identity. Using examples from Icelandic tourism to a beloved San Francisco bakery to the 'Lonely Planet effect,' she illustrates how floods of outsiders can fundamentally alter what made a place special. She concludes that while change is natural, we can be thoughtful about its rate, and that 'invisible tourism'—lurking without participating—offers a way to appreciate communities without destroying them.
key_points: Online communities are more vulnerable to outside attention than physical cities because mobility is frictionless—visiting requires only clicks, not plane tickets
Some communities resist being defined or labeled as a defensive mechanism against colonization by outsiders
Unlike bakeries that produce croissants, online communities 'produce' ideas through member participation, so new members don't just consume—they change the product itself
The internet uniquely allows 'invisible tourism' where you can consume experiences without your presence detracting from others
The question isn't whether to embrace change, but being thoughtful about the rate of change we introduce to fragile ecosystems
money_quotes: Not every community wants to be found.
We don't want to become a 'thing' because 'things' are destroyable by outsiders.
Blasting a fragile ecosystem with a firehose of outsiders, even if they have good intentions, can destroy its essence.
Croissants are passively consumed by paying customers; nobody is jumping over the counter and trying to make the croissants themselves.

One tweet, post, or article from someone with a big enough audience is like sunlight on a magnifying glass, concentrating screaming hordes of people onto a single, unsuspecting point that spontaneously combusts into flames.
Take only pictures, leave only footprints.
When does enthusiasm cross over to exploitation? I'm not sure I know the difference yet, but I know that there is one.
key_insight: Online communities are uniquely fragile because what they produce—ideas and culture—is inseparable from who participates, making thoughtful consideration of when to share and when to lurk an ethical imperative.
